category_title: Sixth-grade students with LD who doodle and lack orientation to the teacher during math
minimal_context: Chris is sitting at his desk at the beginning of math class with his materials out, and the goal is for him to orient toward the teacher to begin the lesson.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Class is starting. Please make sure you have your pencil, paper, and math book ready, and look up here at the board so we can begin the lesson.

STUDENT: (Sitting in his seat with his pencil, paper, and math book on the desk, but looking down and drawing a picture) Hey guys, check out this drawing of the basketball game.

TUTOR: Chris, we are starting math now. Please stop drawing and pay attention to the lesson.

STUDENT: Math is just not my subject.

TUTOR: You need to listen to the instruction so you can understand the concepts we are covering today.

STUDENT: (Continues to doodle on his paper, making a funny face to the peers next to him instead of orienting toward the teacher) I know, when I listen more I get it more easily, but I need to finish this.

options.A: Allow Chris to work at his own pace during the math lesson and take a break from the activity as and when he wants.
options.B: Allow Chris to withdraw from the high-stimulation classroom environment for a time-limited period (Quiet Time) during the math lesson to prevent an emotional overload.
options.C: Meet with Chris right before math class each day to complete the MARS checklist, using its prompts to engage him in preemptive cognitive-motivational strategy use.
options.D: Deliver opportunities to respond (OTRs) consistently to Chris throughout the duration of the math lesson as the instructional concepts are being covered.
